behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: Range parsing (A1:A10 notation), cell lookup (finding A1, A2, ..., A10), and sum aggregation must work together. A unit test of just sum([1,2,3])==6 would miss failures in range resolution or cell reference. Mocking the range or cell values would hide integration bugs in how the formula engine chains these components.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with cells A1–A10 populated with values 1–10. Enter =SUM(A1:A10) in a target cell and verify the result is 55. Change a value (e.g., A1 to 100) and verify the sum recalculates immediately to 154. Test with empty cells, text, and mixed types to verify edge cases.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real failures in range parsing or cell retrieval would slip through a unit test; integration ensures the full evaluation pipeline works.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: Cascaded evaluation requires correct topological ordering of dependencies. If B1 is evaluated before A1 is updated, or if the dependent cell receives a stale value, the result is wrong. Unit testing each formula in isolation (B1 alone, then B1+1) would miss failures in dependency ordering or propagation timing.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1=5, B1==A1*2, C1==B1+1. Verify C1 evaluates to 11. Change A1 to 10 and verify C1 recalculates to 21 (not 11). Test deeper nesting (3–4 levels) and verify no cell receives stale intermediate values. Verify order of evaluation.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Topological ordering bugs in the dependency graph only manifest with multiple cells in a chain; mocking intermediate values would hide real failures.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: While cycle detection can be unit tested as a graph algorithm, the real failure is in the evaluator. If cycle detection doesn't integrate with the evaluation loop, the engine may hang instead of returning an error. Testing the cycle-detection algorithm alone wouldn't catch hangs or incorrect error handling during formula evaluation.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1==B1+1, B1==A1*2 (circular). Attempt to read or edit A1; verify a circular reference error is returned (not a hang, not a stale value). Test multiple circular paths (A→B→C→A). Set a timeout to ensure no infinite loop occurs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A unit test of graph cycle detection would pass, but the real failure (hangs during evaluation) would not be caught without testing the integrated evaluator.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP must resolve a range reference (e.g., A1:C5), search it, and return the correct column. Mocking the lookup table would hide bugs in range resolution or search logic. A unit test of the lookup algorithm alone would not test integration with the cell reference system.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table (A1:C5: names, IDs, values). Enter =VLOOKUP("name", A1:C5, 2, 0) and verify the correct ID is returned. Test with non-existent keys to verify an error is returned (not a null or zero). Test with different column indices (2, 3) and verify correct column is returned.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real failures in range resolution or search would be hidden by mocking the table; the full integration must be tested.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: Nested IF requires the formula parser/evaluator to recursively parse and evaluate nested expressions correctly. A unit test of IF as a standalone function (with pre-evaluated arguments) would not catch bugs in recursive evaluation of nested expressions or incorrect argument evaluation order.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1 and set it to different values (5, 15, 25). For each, enter the nested IF formula and verify the correct result: "low", "medium", "high". Verify the inner IF is evaluated before the outer IF receives its result. Test deeply nested conditions (3+ levels).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Recursive expression evaluation in the formula parser is critical; a unit test of IF logic alone would miss parsing or recursion bugs.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: The function must correctly identify and skip actual empty cells (not mocked). A unit test with mock empty values might not catch bugs in how the engine represents or treats empty cells (null vs. empty string vs. uninitialized).
behaviors.test_description: Create A1="hello", A2=(empty), A3="world". Enter =CONCATENATE(A1,",",A2,",",A3) and verify the result is "hello,,world" (empty cells preserved) or "hello,world" (empty cells skipped), as per spec. Test all-empty, mixed empty, and no-empty scenarios.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real behavior of actual empty cells may differ from mocked empty values; the full cell evaluation context must be tested.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require the engine to resolve sheet names, find the target sheet, and locate the cell within it. Mocking sheet resolution would hide bugs in how sheets are looked up or names are parsed. A unit test of a single sheet would not test the multi-sheet integration.
behaviors.test_description: Create two sheets (Sheet1 and Sheet2). Place value 5 in Sheet2!A1. In Sheet1, enter =Sheet2!A1+1 and verify result is 6. Test with non-existent sheet names to verify error handling. Test with non-existent cells in valid sheets. Verify changes to Sheet2!A1 trigger recalculation in Sheet1.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Sheet resolution is an integration point; mocking it would hide real failures in cross-sheet reference handling.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: Topological recalculation requires the full dependency graph tracking and evaluation engine. Mocking dependency tracking or evaluation order would hide bugs. A unit test of topological sort alone would not test integration with formula evaluation or detect stale values.
behaviors.test_description: Create dependency chain: A1=5, B1==A1*2, C1==B1+1, D1==C1+B1. Change A1 to 10. Verify all cells recalculate in correct order and arrive at final values (B1=20, C1=21, D1=41). Test multi-branch dependencies and verify no cell receives a stale value at any point. Add a probe cell to detect recalculation order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Topological ordering bugs only manifest with multiple dependent cells; mocking the dependency graph would hide real failures.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: Undo must restore both formula text AND dependent values, requiring coordination between undo stack, formula parsing, and dependency evaluation. Mocking any of these components would hide bugs in the actual restoration process or stale values.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1=5, B1==A1*2 (value 10). Edit B1 to ==A1*3. Verify B1 now shows formula ==A1*3 and value 15. Undo the edit. Verify B1 is restored to ==A1*2 with value 10. Test multiple undo steps and verify all dependent values and formulas are correctly restored. Test undo with multiple dependent cells.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Undo must coordinate formula text, values, and dependencies; mocking any component would hide real restoration failures.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: This requires actual file I/O and binary serialization/deserialization. Mocking file I/O would defeat the purpose of testing the real format and precision handling. A unit test cannot verify that the format is correctly implemented or that floating-point precision is preserved across save/load cycles.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with diverse cell types (integers, floats, strings, formulas, empty cells, cross-sheet references). Save to a binary file. Load into a new workbook. Verify all cell values, formulas, and formatting are byte-identical. Test floating-point precision with values like 0.1 + 0.2 to ensure no rounding errors. Verify formula references are preserved (not converted to values).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real failures in serialization format, deserialization, or floating-point precision only surface with actual file I/O; mocking would hide them.
